How Age and Education Level Predict Who is More Likely to Receive a Flu Shot

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

PQHS 432 Project using a sample of 2000 subjects from the Ohio BRFSS 2024

Author

Lauren Sapienza

Published

2026-02-24

R Packages and Setup

Code 
knitr::opts_chunk$set(comment = NA) 

library(janitor) 
library(naniar)

library(broom)
library(car)
library(caret)
library(cutpointr)
library(GGally)
library(haven)
library(kableExtra)
library(mice)
library(patchwork)
library(pROC)
library(readxl)
library(rms)

library(easystats)
library(tidyverse)

theme_set(theme_bw())

1 Data Source

My data are sourced from the 2024 Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) Survey, which are described and can be accessed here.

The BRFSS survey is conducted by state health departments with assistance from the CDC, who aggregates and publishes the final data set each year. The aim is to gather data from adult US residents regarding their risk behaviors and health practices. Surveys are performed monthly over the phone and are divided into a land-line telephone sample and a cellular telephone sample.

2 The Subjects

Our population of interest includes over 6000 subjects with complete data on our logistic regression outcome: during the past 12 months, have you had either a flu vaccine that was sprayed in your nose or a flu shot injected into your arm? The population was created by filtering to subjects that responded that they lived in Ohio, are between 18 and 79 years old, and completed the interview. The population was then randomly sampled to create a final data set with only 2000 subjects.

3 Loading and Tidying the Data

3.1 Loading the Raw Data

Code 
brfss_2024_raw <- read_xpt("LLCP2024.XPT")

3.2 Cleaning the Data

3.2.1 Selecting Variables We’ll Use

Code 
brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_raw |>
  filter(DISPCODE == 1100, `_STATE` == 39, CSTATE1 == 1 | STATERE1 == 1, `_AGE80` < 80) |>
  select(SEQNO, SEXVAR, PRIMINS2, MEDCOST1, ADDEPEV3, EDUCA, `_AGE80`,
         FLUSHOT7, `_URBSTAT`, `_BMI5`, MENTHLTH, GENHLTH) |>
  zap_label()

First we filter the data to subjects who finished the survey, answered they resided in Ohio, and are less than 80 years old. We filter the age like this because all ages above 79 are listed as 80. Then we select our variables of interest and get rid of any labels.

3.2.2 Changing Variable Names and Converting Variable Types

Code 
brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'PRIMINS2', condition = ~ .x == 77) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'PRIMINS2', condition = ~ .x == 99)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'MEDCOST1', condition = ~ .x == 7) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'MEDCOST1', condition = ~ .x == 9)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'ADDEPEV3', condition = ~ .x == 7) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'ADDEPEV3', condition = ~ .x == 9)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'EDUCA', condition = ~ .x == 9)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'FLUSHOT7', condition = ~ .x == 7) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'FLUSHOT7', condition = ~ .x == 9)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'MENTHLTH', condition = ~ .x == 77) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'MENTHLTH', condition = ~ .x == 99)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'GENTHLTH', condition = ~ .x == 77) |>
  replace_with_na_at(.vars = 'GENTHLTH', condition = ~ .x == 99)

Now we change survey answers coded as ‘Don’t Know’, ‘Refused’ or similar to missing values.

Code 
brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(sex = case_when(SEXVAR == 1 ~ "Male",
                         SEXVAR == 2 ~ "Female")) |> 
  mutate(sex = factor(sex))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(insur = case_when(PRIMINS2 == 1 | PRIMINS2 == 2 ~ "Private",
                           PRIMINS2 == 3 | PRIMINS2 == 4 ~ "Medicare",
                           PRIMINS2 == 5 ~ "Medicaid",
                           PRIMINS2 == 6 | PRIMINS2 == 7 | PRIMINS2 == 8 |
                           PRIMINS2 == 9 | PRIMINS2 == 10 | PRIMINS2 == 88 ~ "Other")) |> 
  mutate(insur = factor(insur))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(afford = case_when(MEDCOST1 == 1 ~ "Yes",
                            MEDCOST1 == 2 ~ "No")) |> 
  mutate(afford = factor(afford))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(depr = case_when(ADDEPEV3 == 1 ~ "Yes",
                          ADDEPEV3 == 2 ~ "No")) |> 
  mutate(depr = factor(depr))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(educ = case_when(EDUCA == 1 | EDUCA == 2 |
                          EDUCA == 3 | EDUCA == 4 ~ "HS_or_less",
                          EDUCA == 5 ~ "Some_College",
                          EDUCA == 6 ~ "College_Grad")) |> 
  mutate(educ = factor(educ))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(flushot = case_when(FLUSHOT7 == 1 ~ "Yes",
                             FLUSHOT7 == 2 ~ "No")) |> 
  mutate(flushot = factor(flushot))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(bmi = `_BMI5`/ 100)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(age = `_AGE80`)

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(seqno = as.character(SEQNO))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(rural = case_when(`_URBSTAT` == 1 ~ "Urban",
                           `_URBSTAT` == 2 ~ "Rural")) |> 
  mutate(rural = factor(rural))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |> 
  mutate(genhlth = case_when(GENHLTH == 1 ~ "Excellent",
                             GENHLTH == 2 ~ "Very_Good",
                             GENHLTH == 3 ~ "Good",
                             GENHLTH == 4 ~ "Fair",
                             GENHLTH == 5 ~ "Poor")) |> 
  mutate(genhlth = factor(genhlth))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  mutate(menthlth = if_else(MENTHLTH == 88, 0, MENTHLTH))

We also recode all the numeric values to their associated categories using the BRFSS 2024 code book. We also take this opportunity to rename the variables, collapse some categories, and make sure all the variables are the correct data type.

3.2.3 Working with Catergorial Predictors

Code 
brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  mutate(insur = fct_relevel(insur, "Private", "Medicare", "Medicaid", "Other"))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  mutate(educ = fct_relevel(educ, "HS_or_less", "Some_College", "College_Grad"))

brfss_2024_temp <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  mutate(genhlth = fct_relevel(genhlth, "Excellent", "Very_Good", "Good", "Fair", "Poor"))

Here we relevel some of the factor variables so they are in a sensible order.

3.2.4 Arranging the Tibble

Code 
brfss_2024_final <- brfss_2024_temp |>
  select(seqno, menthlth, flushot, age, sex, bmi,
         genhlth, depr, insur, afford, educ, rural) |>
  droplevels() |>
  janitor::clean_names()

brfss_2024_final <- brfss_2024_final |> filter(complete.cases(flushot))

We now select our new variables and put them in order so that our ID code (seqno) is first. We then drop any extra levels and filter to complete cases of our logistic regression outcome.

3.2.5 Sampling the Data

Code 
set.seed(432)

brfss_2024 <- brfss_2024_final |> slice_sample(n=2000, replace=FALSE)

Last, we take a sample of our larger data set so that we only have 2000 subjects in our final tibble.

4 The Tidy Tibble

4.1 Listing the Tibble

Code 
brfss_2024
# A tibble: 2,000 × 12
   seqno     menthlth flushot   age sex     bmi genhlth depr  insur afford educ 
   <chr>        <dbl> <fct>   <dbl> <fct> <dbl> <fct>   <fct> <fct> <fct>  <fct>
 1 20240009…        0 Yes        72 Fema…  36.1 Good    No    Medi… No     Some…
 2 20240043…        0 No         78 Male   26.3 Fair    No    Other Yes    HS_o…
 3 20240046…       15 Yes        56 Male   30   Good    Yes   Other No     Coll…
 4 20240050…        0 Yes        69 Male   29.8 Good    No    Medi… No     Coll…
 5 20240096…        0 Yes        50 Fema…  NA   Excell… No    Priv… No     Some…
 6 20240060…        0 No         45 Male   30.5 Good    No    Priv… No     Some…
 7 20240057…        0 No         35 Fema…  37.1 Good    No    Priv… No     Some…
 8 20240077…       30 No         57 Male   15.9 Poor    Yes   Medi… No     Some…
 9 20240031…        4 Yes        26 Male   21.3 Excell… No    Priv… No     HS_o…
10 20240004…       10 Yes        65 Male   35.5 Fair    Yes   Medi… No     Coll…
# ℹ 1,990 more rows
# ℹ 1 more variable: rural <fct>

4.2 Size and Identifiers

Our analytic tibble, called brfss_2024, has 2000 rows (observations) on 12 columns (variables). Our indicator variable is seqno, and we have a unique seqno for each row in our data set, as the code below demonstrates.

Code 
identical(nrow(brfss_2024), n_distinct(brfss_2024$seqno))
[1] TRUE

4.3 Saving as an Rds file

Code 
write_rds(brfss_2024, "brfss_2024.Rds")

5 The Code Book

5.1 Five Key Statements

  1. Sample Size: The data in our complete brfss_2024 sample consist of 2000 subjects from the 2024 BRFSS between the ages of 18 and 79 in whom our logistic outcome variable (flushot) was measured.
  2. Missingness: Of the 2000 subjects, 1781 have complete data on all variables listed below.
  3. Our outcome variables are menthlth, which is the number of days in the last 30 the subject reported having poor mental health, for the linear regression, and flushot, which indicates whether or not the subject had a flu shot in the last 12 months, for the logistic regression. We are missing data for menthlth, as it will be filtered separately during the linear regression.
  4. Candidate predictors for my models include age, sex, bmi, genhlth, and depr for the linear model and age, insur, afford, educ, and rural for the logistic model.
  5. The only other variable contained in my tidy tibble is seqno which is the subject identifying code.

5.2 Describing the Variables

  • Definitions
  • data_codebook()
  • describe
  • Missingness
Variable Role Type Description
seqno identifier - BRFSS subject identifying code
menthlth outcome (linear) quant For how many days during the past 30 days was your mental health not good? Number of days, 0-30
flushot outcome (logistic) binary During the past 12 months, have you had either a flu vaccine that was sprayed in your nose or a flu shot injected into your arm? (Yes/No)
age predictor quant Imputed1 age in years, 18-79
sex predictor binary Sex of respondent, Male or Female
bmi predictor quant Body Mass Index, kg/m2
genhlth predictor 5-cat Would you say that in general your health is: Excellent, Very Good, Good, Fair, Poor?
depr predictor binary Ever told you had a depressive disorder? (Yes/No)
insur predictor 4-cat What is the current primary source of your health care coverage? Four categories: Private, Medicare, Medicaid, Other
afford predictor binary Was there a time in the past 12 months when you needed to see a doctor but could not because you could not afford it? (Yes/No)
educ predictor 3-cat What is the highest grade or year of school you completed? Three categories: High School or less, Some College, College Graduate
rural predictor binary Urban/Rural status of county where subject lives, Urban or Rural
Code 
data_codebook(brfss_2024 |> select(-seqno))
select(brfss_2024, -seqno) (2000 rows and 11 variables, 11 shown)

ID | Name     | Type        |   Missings |         Values |            N
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
1  | menthlth | numeric     |  25 (1.2%) |        [0, 30] |         1975
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
2  | flushot  | categorical |   0 (0.0%) |             No | 1098 (54.9%)
   |          |             |            |            Yes |  902 (45.1%)
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
3  | age      | numeric     |   0 (0.0%) |       [18, 79] |         2000
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
4  | sex      | categorical |   0 (0.0%) |         Female | 1066 (53.3%)
   |          |             |            |           Male |  934 (46.7%)
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
5  | bmi      | numeric     | 120 (6.0%) | [14.53, 73.18] |         1880
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
6  | genhlth  | categorical |   2 (0.1%) |      Excellent |  251 (12.6%)
   |          |             |            |      Very_Good |  661 (33.1%)
   |          |             |            |           Good |  678 (33.9%)
   |          |             |            |           Fair |  307 (15.4%)
   |          |             |            |           Poor |  101 ( 5.1%)
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
7  | depr     | categorical |  10 (0.5%) |             No | 1443 (72.5%)
   |          |             |            |            Yes |  547 (27.5%)
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
8  | insur    | categorical |  87 (4.3%) |        Private |  897 (46.9%)
   |          |             |            |       Medicare |  635 (33.2%)
   |          |             |            |       Medicaid |  162 ( 8.5%)
   |          |             |            |          Other |  219 (11.4%)
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
9  | afford   | categorical |   5 (0.2%) |             No | 1837 (92.1%)
   |          |             |            |            Yes |  158 ( 7.9%)

10 | educ     | categorical |   3 (0.1%) |     HS_or_less |  713 (35.7%)
   |          |             |            |   Some_College |  529 (26.5%)
   |          |             |            |   College_Grad |  755 (37.8%)
---+----------+-------------+------------+----------------+-------------
11 | rural    | categorical |   0 (0.0%) |          Rural |  156 ( 7.8%)
   |          |             |            |          Urban | 1844 (92.2%)
------------------------------------------------------------------------

# A tibble: 4 × 3

  1. Already imputed age was the only option for a continuous age variable in the BRFSS data set.↩︎

6 Linear Regression Design

6.1 My First Research Question

How well can you predict the number of poor mental health days someone has had using demographic factors such as sex and age, and health factors like self-reported overall health, BMI, and a previous depression diagnosis, in a sample of 679 subjects from the BRFSS 2024?

6.2 My Quantitative Outcome

Code 
brfss_linear <- brfss_2024 |> 
  filter(menthlth > 0 & menthlth < 30) |>
  filter(complete.cases(menthlth))

First, we filter our outcome so we are only looking at values from 1-29, as 0 and 30 both contain over 20% of the data if we include them. We then filter to complete cases.

Code 
p1 <- ggplot(brfss_linear, aes(x = menthlth)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, col = "white", fill = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Histogram of menthlt", x = "Poor Mental Health Days")

p2 <- ggplot(brfss_linear, aes(sample = menthlth)) +
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line(col = "red") +
  labs(y = "Poor Mental Health Days", x = "Standard Normal Distribution",
    title = "Normal Q-Q plot")

p3 <- ggplot(brfss_linear, aes(x = menthlth, y = "")) +
  geom_violin(fill = "lightblue", width = 1.1) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 16, col = "red") +
  labs(y = "", x = "Poor Mental Health Days", title = "Boxplot with Violin")

p1 + (p2 / p3 + plot_layout(heights = c(2, 1))) +
  plot_annotation(title = "menthlth distribution")

6.2.1 Numerical Summary of my linear outcome

Code 
Hmisc::describe(brfss_linear$menthlth)
brfss_linear$menthlth 
       n  missing distinct     Info     Mean  pMedian      Gmd      .05 
     679        0       24    0.988    8.099      7.5    7.732        1 
     .10      .25      .50      .75      .90      .95 
       1        2        5       14       20       25 

lowest :  1  2  3  4  5, highest: 24 25 27 28 29
Code 
mosaic::favstats(brfss_linear$menthlth)
 min Q1 median Q3 max     mean       sd   n missing
   1  2      5 14  29 8.098675 7.282783 679       0
Code 
brfss_linear |> tabyl(menthlth) |> adorn_pct_formatting() |>
  arrange(desc(n)) |> head(5)
 menthlth   n percent
        2 111   16.3%
        5  94   13.8%
        1  78   11.5%
        3  74   10.9%
       10  71   10.5%

My outcome does not meet the rules given in the instructions as my outcome has values that exceed 10% of the data, as I thought my outcome was fine based on discussion in class and the check after Lab 4. I felt it was too late to change my outcome by the time I noticed this rule in the example project, so I apologize for this.

6.2.2 Summary Statements about my outcome, menthlth

My quantitative outcome is menthlth which represents the number of days in the last 30 that the subject had poor mental health. I am interested in exploring what variables have the strongest association with menthlth and how well we can predict menthlth from these variables.

  • After filtering from the brfss_2024 tibble to values of menthlth between 1 and 29, we have 679 subjects with complete data on the outcome.
  • menthlth has 24 distinct values.
  • The range is 1 to 29, with mean 8.099 days and standard deviation 7.282 days.
  • The most common value for menthlth is 2, which occurs in 16.3% of my subjects.
  • The distribution is skewed pretty heavily to the right, with a median of 5, as shown in the plots above. There also seems to be a bias for round numbers because of the nature of the survey question, which can be seen in the histogram spike around 15. Our distribution is heavy tailed, especially on the lower end of the range.

6.3 Linear Model Predictor Candidates

The predictors I intend to use in my linear model are age and bmi, which are quantitative, sex and depr, which are binary categorical variables, and genhlth which is a 5-category variable in my brfss_linear tibble.

  • age has 62 distinct values, and is measured in years.
  • bmi has 402 distinct values, and is measured in kg/m2. bmi has 37 missing values.
  • sex has two levels, with 415 female and 264 male subjects.
  • depr also has two levels, with 295 subjects answering “Yes” and 377 answering “No”. depr has 7 missing values.
  • genhlth’s five categories have at least 30 observations in each level, as we can see in the table below.
Code 
brfss_linear |> select(genhlth) |> summary()
      genhlth   
 Excellent: 65  
 Very_Good:210  
 Good     :233  
 Fair     :138  
 Poor     : 33

6.3.1 Anticipated Direction of Effects

I believe that a larger amount of poor mental health days will be associated with lower age, higher bmi, with females, with ‘yes’ for a depression diagnosis, and with lower self reported overall health.

6.3.2 Missingness Summary

Code 
linear_model_predictors <- brfss_linear |> select(age, sex, bmi, genhlth, depr)

miss_var_summary(linear_model_predictors) |> filter(n_miss > 0)
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  variable n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>     <int>    <num>
1 bmi          37     5.45
2 depr          7     1.03
Code 
miss_case_table(linear_model_predictors)
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  n_miss_in_case n_cases pct_cases
           <int>   <int>     <dbl>
1              0     635     93.5 
2              1      44      6.48
  • I have complete data for all of the linear model predictors in 635 (93.5%) of the 679 rows in my data.
  • I am missing 37 values (5.45%) for bmi.
  • I am missing 7 values (1.03%) for depr.

7 Logistic Regression Design

7.1 My Second Research Question

What variables have the largest effect size when predicting whether or not a person has had a flu shot, from the variables of age, education level, living in a rural or urban county, insurance type, and whether the person can afford to see a doctor, in a sample of 2000 subjects from the 2024 BRFSS?

7.2 My Binary Outcome

Code 
brfss_2024 <- brfss_2024 |>
  mutate(flushot = case_when(flushot == 'Yes' ~ 1,
                             flushot == 'No' ~ 0))

Here we recode flushot so that answers of ‘Yes’ are 1 and answers of ‘No’ are 0.

My quantitative outcome is flushot which represents whether or not the subject had a flu shot in the last 12 months. I am interested to see if the variables I picked are able to predict flushot well, and which ones will have the greatest association with flushot. I have complete data for all 2000 subjects and over 150 subjects in the smaller of my groups.

Code 
brfss_2024 |> tabyl(flushot) |> adorn_pct_formatting()
 flushot    n percent
       0 1098   54.9%
       1  902   45.1%

7.3 Logistic Model Predictor Candidates

The predictors I intend to use in my logistic model are age which is quantitative and was previously described, insur and educ, which are categorical variables with 4 and 3 levels respectively, and rural and afford which are binary category variables.

  • insur has 4 levels with at least 30 observations in each level, as we can see in the table below. insur has 87 missing values.
  • educ has 3 levels with at least 30 observations in each level, as we can see in the table below. educ has 3 missing values.
  • rural has two levels, with 156 subjects living in rural counties and 1844 subjects living in urban counties.
  • afford has two levels with 158 subjects answering ‘Yes’ and 1837 subjects answering ‘No’. afford has 5 missing values.
Code 
brfss_2024 |> tabyl(insur, flushot)
    insur   0   1
  Private 511 386
 Medicare 279 356
 Medicaid 109  53
    Other 143  76
     <NA>  56  31
Code 
brfss_2024 |> tabyl(educ, flushot)
         educ   0   1
   HS_or_less 461 252
 Some_College 312 217
 College_Grad 324 431
         <NA>   1   2

7.3.1 Anticipated Direction of Effects

I believe that higher rates of having a flu shot will be associated with higher age, Medicare for insurance, higher education levels, subjects living in urban counties, and with being able to always afford to go to the doctor.

7.3.2 Missingness Summary

Code 
logistic_model_predictors <- brfss_2024 |> select(age, insur, educ, rural, afford)

miss_var_summary(logistic_model_predictors) |> filter(n_miss > 0)
# A tibble: 3 × 3
  variable n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>     <int>    <num>
1 insur        87     4.35
2 afford        5     0.25
3 educ          3     0.15
Code 
miss_case_table(logistic_model_predictors)
# A tibble: 3 × 3
  n_miss_in_case n_cases pct_cases
           <int>   <int>     <dbl>
1              0    1906     95.3 
2              1      93      4.65
3              2       1      0.05
  • I have complete data for all of the logistic model predictors in 1906 (95.3%) of the 2000 rows in my data.
  • I am missing 87 values (4.35%) for insur.
  • I am missing 5 values (0.25%) for afford.
  • I am missing 3 values (0.15%) for educ.

8 Linear Regression Analyses

8.1 Missingness

I will be assuming my predictor variables are missing at random and so I will be using single imputation for my analysis. I am also assuming my outcome, menthlth, is missing completely at random, so we filtered to complete cases earlier.

Code 
miss_var_summary(linear_model_predictors) |> filter(n_miss > 0)
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  variable n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>     <int>    <num>
1 bmi          37     5.45
2 depr          7     1.03

I am missing data on two of my predictors before imputation, bmi and depr.

Code 
brfss_lin_imp <- 
  mice(brfss_linear, m = 1, seed = 432, print = FALSE) |>
  complete() |>
  tibble()
Warning: Number of logged events: 1
Code 
n_miss(brfss_lin_imp)
[1] 0

After imputation, we quickly check that we have no missing data, which is true.

8.2 Outcome Transformation

Code 
mod_temp <- lm(menthlth ~ age + sex + bmi + genhlth + depr,
               data = brfss_lin_imp)

boxCox(mod_temp)

The Box-Cox plot suggests using a logarithm transformation, so we will check the plots of the distribution and compare to the untransformed plots from Section 6.2.

Code 
brfss_lin_imp <- brfss_lin_imp |>
  mutate(log_ment = log(menthlth))

p1 <- ggplot(brfss_lin_imp, aes(x = log_ment)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, col = "white", fill = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Histogram of log(menthlt)", x = "Log of menthlth (days)")

p2 <- ggplot(brfss_lin_imp, aes(sample = log_ment)) +
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line(col = "red") +
  labs(y = "Log of menthlth (days)", x = "Standard Normal Distribution",
    title = "Normal Q-Q plot")

p3 <- ggplot(brfss_lin_imp, aes(x = log_ment, y = "")) +
  geom_violin(fill = "lightblue") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.3) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 16, col = "red") +
  labs(y = "", x = "Log of menthlth (days)", title = "Boxplot with Violin")

p1 + (p2 / p3 + plot_layout(heights = c(2, 1))) +
  plot_annotation(title = "log(menthlth) distribution")

Our distribution appears to be more normally distributed after the transformation, especially looking at the box plot, so we will continue with using the transformed outcome. However, our histogram and normal Q-Q plot still show some issues with normality.

8.3 Scatterplot Matrix and Collinearity

Now we can look at the scatter plot matrix after transformation and imputation.

Code 
ggpairs(brfss_lin_imp, columns = c("age", "bmi", "sex", 
                               "genhlth", "depr", "log_ment"))

It does not look like we will have any issues with collinearity, but we quickly check the generalized VIF to make sure.

Code 
modelA <- lm(log_ment ~ age + bmi + sex + genhlth + depr,
             data = brfss_lin_imp)

car::vif(modelA)
            GVIF Df GVIF^(1/(2*Df))
age     1.081864  1        1.040127
bmi     1.100415  1        1.049006
sex     1.052734  1        1.026028
genhlth 1.248851  4        1.028167
depr    1.095112  1        1.046476

None of our generalized VIF values are large enough to be of concern, so we are good to continue.

8.4 Model A

8.4.1 Fitting Model A

Code 
modelA_lm <- lm(log_ment ~ age + bmi + sex + genhlth + depr,
                data = brfss_lin_imp)

dd <- datadist(brfss_lin_imp)
options(datadist = "dd")

modelA_ols <- ols(log_ment ~ age + bmi + sex + genhlth + depr,
                  data = brfss_lin_imp, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

We fit using both lm and ols so we can use them with different functions later on. For model A we are using all predictor variables: age, bmi, sex, genhlth, and depr.

8.4.2 Tidied Coefficient Estimates (Model A)

Code 
model_parameters(modelA_lm, ci = 0.90) |> print_md(digits = 3)
Parameter Coefficient SE 90% CI t(670) p
(Intercept) 1.508 0.203 (1.174, 1.843) 7.423 < .001
age -0.010 0.002 (-0.014, -0.007) -4.675 < .001
bmi 0.003 0.005 (-0.005, 0.011) 0.577 0.564
sex (Male) -0.154 0.074 (-0.276, -0.032) -2.082 0.038
genhlth (Very_Good) 0.245 0.132 (0.028, 0.462) 1.862 0.063
genhlth (Good) 0.464 0.134 (0.244, 0.684) 3.470 < .001
genhlth (Fair) 0.750 0.148 (0.505, 0.994) 5.051 < .001
genhlth (Poor) 1.080 0.203 (0.745, 1.415) 5.312 < .001
depr (Yes) 0.405 0.074 (0.282, 0.527) 5.450 < .001

Looking at the variable of age, if we have two subjects with the same values of bmi, sex, genhlth, and depr, but subject A has a value of age that is 1 year larger than subject B, our model A predicts that subject A’s outcome log(menthlth) will be 0.01 less than subject B. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with a difference in our outcome log(menthlth) falling between -0.014 and -0.007, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model A is correct.

8.4.3 Summarizing Fit (Model A)

Code 
model_performance(modelA_lm) |> print_md(digits = 3)
Indices of model performance
AIC AICc BIC R2 R2 (adj.) RMSE Sigma
1821.2 1821.6 1866.4 0.153 0.143 0.912 0.918
Code 
glance(modelA_lm) |>
  select(r2 = r.squared, adjr2 = adj.r.squared, sigma, 
         AIC, BIC, nobs, df, df.residual) |>
  kable(digits = c(3, 3, 3, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0))
r2 adjr2 sigma AIC BIC nobs df df.residual
0.153 0.143 0.918 1821.2 1866.4 679 8 670

Our adjusted R2 is pretty close to zero, which implies that our variables are not accounting for much of the variation of our data. We can also see that we are using 8 degrees of freedom in model A.

8.4.4 Regression Diagnostics (Model A)

Code 
check_model(modelA_lm, detrend = FALSE)

Our assumptions of linearity and homogeneity seem to hold up well, with perhaps some minor issues at both ends. We do not have any influential observations or issues with collinearity. Our normality plot also looks okay, but it looks like there may be some issues with normality at the ends. Our posterior predictive check does not look good at all, with most of the data not lining up well with the predicted data.

8.5 Non-Linearity

Code 
plot(spearman2(log_ment ~ age + bmi + sex + genhlth + depr,
                data = brfss_lin_imp))

It looks like depr and genhlth will have the greatest impact on the model as nonlinear terms, so we will create an interaction between them that adds an additional 4 degrees of freedom. bmi doesn’t has as big of an impact according to our plot, but as it is the next best I will add a 4-degree restricted cubic spline, which has 3 degrees of freedom.

8.6 Model B

Model B will contain two non-linear terms, adding up to an additional 6 degrees of freedom, to model A. Model B will also continue to use all 5 of our selected predictors.

8.6.1 Fitting Model B

Code 
modelB_lm <- lm(log_ment ~ age + rcs(bmi, 4) + sex + genhlth * depr,
                data = brfss_lin_imp)

dd <- datadist(brfss_lin_imp)
options(datadist = "dd")

modelB_ols <- ols(log_ment ~ age + rcs(bmi, 4) + sex + genhlth * depr,
                data = brfss_lin_imp, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

Once again, we fit with both lm and ols.

8.6.2 Tidied Coefficient Estimates (Model B)

Code 
model_parameters(modelB_lm, ci = 0.90) |> print_md(digits = 3)
Parameter Coefficient SE 90% CI t(664) p
(Intercept) 1.721 0.620 (0.700, 2.743) 2.776 0.006
age -0.010 0.002 (-0.014, -0.006) -4.557 < .001
rcs(bmi ( degree) -0.010 0.027 (-0.054, 0.035) -0.355 0.722
rcs(bmi ( degree) 0.067 0.133 (-0.152, 0.286) 0.502 0.616
rcs(bmi ( degree) -0.157 0.316 (-0.677, 0.364) -0.496 0.620
sex (Male) -0.144 0.075 (-0.267, -0.020) -1.917 0.056
genhlth (Very_Good) 0.329 0.154 (0.075, 0.583) 2.131 0.033
genhlth (Good) 0.500 0.158 (0.240, 0.760) 3.169 0.002
genhlth (Fair) 0.777 0.192 (0.460, 1.094) 4.039 < .001
genhlth (Poor) 1.460 0.300 (0.966, 1.954) 4.866 < .001
depr (Yes) 0.657 0.266 (0.218, 1.095) 2.468 0.014
genhlth (Very_Good) × depr (Yes) -0.312 0.297 (-0.801, 0.177) -1.050 0.294
genhlth (Good) × depr (Yes) -0.203 0.292 (-0.685, 0.278) -0.696 0.487
genhlth (Fair) × depr (Yes) -0.216 0.312 (-0.729, 0.298) -0.691 0.490
genhlth (Poor) × depr (Yes) -0.752 0.426 (-1.454, -0.051) -1.767 0.078

Looking at the variable of sex, if we have two subjects with the same values of bmi, age, genhlth, and depr, but subject A has a sex of male and subject B has a sex of female, our model B predicts that subject A’s outcome log(menthlth) will be 0.144 less subject B. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with a difference in our outcome log(menthlth) falling between -0.267 and -0.020, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model B is correct.

8.6.3 Effects Plot for Model B

Code 
summary(modelB_ols, conf.int = 0.90) |> kable(digits = 3)
Low High Diff. Effect S.E. Lower 0.9 Upper 0.9 Type
age 36.000 65.000 29.00 -0.291 0.064 -0.396 -0.186 1
bmi 24.305 33.285 8.98 0.057 0.090 -0.092 0.206 1
sex - Male:Female 1.000 2.000 NA -0.144 0.075 -0.267 -0.020 1
genhlth - Excellent:Good 3.000 1.000 NA -0.500 0.158 -0.760 -0.240 1
genhlth - Very_Good:Good 3.000 2.000 NA -0.171 0.115 -0.360 0.019 1
genhlth - Fair:Good 3.000 4.000 NA 0.277 0.154 0.023 0.531 1
genhlth - Poor:Good 3.000 5.000 NA 0.960 0.278 0.501 1.418 1
depr - Yes:No 1.000 2.000 NA 0.453 0.123 0.251 0.655 1
Code 
plot(summary(modelB_ols, conf.int = 0.90))

It looks like the best and worst levels of genhlth have pretty large effect sizes when corrected for depr = No. depr is also has a pretty large effect size when corrected for genhlth = Good.

8.6.4 Summarizing Fit (Model B)

Code 
model_performance(modelB_lm) |> print_md(digits = 3)
Indices of model performance
AIC AICc BIC R2 R2 (adj.) RMSE Sigma
1829.4 1830.2 1901.7 0.157 0.140 0.909 0.919
Code 
glance(modelB_lm) |>
  select(r2 = r.squared, adjr2 = adj.r.squared, sigma, 
         AIC, BIC, nobs, df, df.residual) |>
  kable(digits = c(3, 3, 3, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0))
r2 adjr2 sigma AIC BIC nobs df df.residual
0.157 0.14 0.919 1829.4 1901.7 679 14 664

Our performance for model B doesn’t appear to be too different from model A, however model B has a higher AIC, BIC, and a lower adjusted R2 than model A. We also see that model B uses 14 degrees of freedom, 6 more than model A like we wanted.

8.6.5 Regression Diagnostics (Model B)

Code 
check_model(modelB_lm, detrend = FALSE)

Everything here looks similar to model A with no problems with assumptions of linearity or homogeneity of variance and no influential observations. It still looks like there may be some issues with normality and the posterior predictive check is just as bad as model A. The collinearity was introduced by our interaction term, so there are no concerns there.

8.7 Validating Models A and B

Code 
set.seed(4321); (valA <- validate(modelA_ols, method = "boot", B = 300))
          index.orig training   test optimism index.corrected   Lower  Upper
R-square      0.1527   0.1597 0.1415   0.0182          0.1345  0.0771 0.1888
MSE           0.8310   0.8203 0.8419  -0.0217          0.8527  0.7787 0.9246
g             0.4411   0.4472 0.4275   0.0197          0.4214  0.3441 0.5069
Intercept     0.0000   0.0000 0.0578  -0.0578          0.0578 -0.3833 0.3794
Slope         1.0000   1.0000 0.9638   0.0362          0.9638  0.7737 1.2256
            n
R-square  300
MSE       300
g         300
Intercept 300
Slope     300
Code 
set.seed(4322); (valB <- validate(modelB_ols, method = "boot", B = 300))
          index.orig training   test optimism index.corrected   Lower  Upper
R-square      0.1575   0.1755 0.1387   0.0368          0.1207  0.0610 0.1724
MSE           0.8263   0.8067 0.8447  -0.0380          0.8643  0.7923 0.9284
g             0.4495   0.4706 0.4256   0.0450          0.4045  0.3311 0.4738
Intercept     0.0000   0.0000 0.1551  -0.1551          0.1551 -0.1728 0.4277
Slope         1.0000   1.0000 0.9062   0.0938          0.9062  0.7473 1.0976
            n
R-square  300
MSE       300
g         300
Intercept 300
Slope     300

It looks like model A has a higher validated R2 and MSE than model B.

8.7.1 Validated \(R^2\) and other statistics

Model Validated \(R^2\) Validated MSE AIC BIC df
A 0.1345 0.8527 1821.2 1866.4 8
B 0.1207 0.8643 1829.4 1901.7 14

8.8 Final Linear Regression Model

I prefer model A because it does better than model B in validated R2, validated MSE, AIC, and BIC. Overall, model A beats model B in most statistics while being simpler than the augmented model B. They also performed very similarly in the check_model plots, so it seems that model A is the better pick.

8.8.1 Winning Model’s Parameter Estimates

Code 
modelA_ols
Linear Regression Model

ols(formula = log_ment ~ age + bmi + sex + genhlth + depr, data = brfss_lin_imp, 
    x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

                Model Likelihood    Discrimination    
                      Ratio Test           Indexes    
Obs     679    LR chi2    112.48    R2       0.153    
sigma0.9177    d.f.            8    R2 adj   0.143    
d.f.    670    Pr(> chi2) 0.0000    g        0.441    

Residuals

     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.35540 -0.69246  0.03234  0.72572  2.27232 

                  Coef    S.E.   t     Pr(>|t|)
Intercept          1.5083 0.2032  7.42 <0.0001 
age               -0.0101 0.0022 -4.68 <0.0001 
bmi                0.0029 0.0050  0.58 0.5644  
sex=Male          -0.1543 0.0741 -2.08 0.0378  
genhlth=Very_Good  0.2453 0.1317  1.86 0.0630  
genhlth=Good       0.4639 0.1337  3.47 0.0006  
genhlth=Fair       0.7495 0.1484  5.05 <0.0001 
genhlth=Poor       1.0803 0.2034  5.31 <0.0001 
depr=Yes           0.4046 0.0742  5.45 <0.0001

In addition to the R2 and adjusted R2 values presented above, our validated R2, as calculated in the previous section, is 0.1345.

Code 
model_parameters(modelA_lm, ci = 0.90) |> print_md(digits = 3)
Parameter Coefficient SE 90% CI t(670) p
(Intercept) 1.508 0.203 (1.174, 1.843) 7.423 < .001
age -0.010 0.002 (-0.014, -0.007) -4.675 < .001
bmi 0.003 0.005 (-0.005, 0.011) 0.577 0.564
sex (Male) -0.154 0.074 (-0.276, -0.032) -2.082 0.038
genhlth (Very_Good) 0.245 0.132 (0.028, 0.462) 1.862 0.063
genhlth (Good) 0.464 0.134 (0.244, 0.684) 3.470 < .001
genhlth (Fair) 0.750 0.148 (0.505, 0.994) 5.051 < .001
genhlth (Poor) 1.080 0.203 (0.745, 1.415) 5.312 < .001
depr (Yes) 0.405 0.074 (0.282, 0.527) 5.450 < .001

It looks like in our final model that age, and a sex of male are negatively related to our outcome log(menthlth). bmi, a depr value of ‘Yes’, and genhlth values of ‘Very Good’, ‘Good’, ‘Fair’, and ‘Poor’ are positively related to the outcome log(menthlth).

8.8.2 Effects Plot for Winning Model

Code 
plot(summary(modelA_ols, conf.int = 0.90))

From this plot we can see how genhlth and depr seem to have some of the greatest effects on outcome. age and sex have more modest effects, while bmi seems to be having the least effect.

8.8.3 Numerical Description of Effect Sizes

Code 
summary(modelA_ols, conf.int = 0.90) |> kable(digits = 3)
Low High Diff. Effect S.E. Lower 0.9 Upper 0.9 Type
age 36.000 65.000 29.00 -0.293 0.063 -0.396 -0.190 1
bmi 24.305 33.285 8.98 0.026 0.045 -0.049 0.101 1
sex - Male:Female 1.000 2.000 NA -0.154 0.074 -0.276 -0.032 1
genhlth - Excellent:Good 3.000 1.000 NA -0.464 0.134 -0.684 -0.244 1
genhlth - Very_Good:Good 3.000 2.000 NA -0.219 0.089 -0.365 -0.072 1
genhlth - Fair:Good 3.000 4.000 NA 0.286 0.101 0.120 0.452 1
genhlth - Poor:Good 3.000 5.000 NA 0.616 0.173 0.332 0.901 1
depr - Yes:No 1.000 2.000 NA 0.405 0.074 0.282 0.527 1

Looking at depr, if we have two subjects with the same age, sex, bmi, and genhlth, but subject A has a depr value of ‘Yes’ and subject B has a depr value of ‘No’, then our model predicts that subject A will have an outcome log(menthlth) 0.405 less than subject B. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with a difference in our outcome log(menthlth) falling between 0.282 and 0.527, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model A is correct.

8.8.4 Nomogram of Winning Model

Code 
plot(nomogram(modelA_ols, fun = exp, funlabel = "menthlth"))

By back transforming our outcome on the nomogram, it is easier to see the effects of each variable on the outcome. We can now see properly that age is negatively related to menhlth while bmi is positively related to menthlth. A sex value of ‘Female’ and a depr value of ‘Yes’ also lead to higher predictions of menthlth. We can also see that the lower levels of genhlth are associated with higher menthlth. We can also see the logarithmic nature of our outcome quite clearly.

I was surprised to see that age had a decently large effect on the outcome, I would have expected it to be similar to bmi. Two variables I thought would have a large effect, depr and genhlth do display large effects on menthlth.

8.8.5 Prediction for a New Subject

Code 
ggplot(Predict(modelA_ols))

In our prediction plot, we can see that the age variable has a larger slope than bmi, which remains mostly flat. bmi also does not really take advantage of the restricted cubic spline we added, which isn’t entirely surprising considering how low it was on the Spearman \(\rho^2\) plot. We also see that there is a larger difference between values of depr than the other categorical variables, and the way that log(menthlth) increases as genhlth becomes worse.

Code 
new_subject <- 
  data.frame(age = c(25), bmi = c(40), sex = c('Female'),
             genhlth = c('Very_Good'), depr = c('Yes'))

preds1 <- predict.lm(modelA_lm, newdata = new_subject, 
                     interval = "prediction", level = 0.90)

exp(preds1)
       fit      lwr      upr
1 7.556179 1.649193 34.62049
age bmi sex genhlth depr estimate (90% CI)
25 40 Female Very Good Yes 7.56 (1.65, 34.62)

Our prediction interval here is not great, as it almost spans our entire range of values for menthlth, which was 1-29.

9 Logistic Regression Analyses

9.1 Missingness

I will be assuming my predictor variables are missing at random and so I will be using single imputation for my analysis. I am also assuming my outcome, flushot, is missing completely at random, so we filtered to complete cases earlier.

Code 
miss_var_summary(logistic_model_predictors) |> filter(n_miss > 0)
# A tibble: 3 × 3
  variable n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>     <int>    <num>
1 insur        87     4.35
2 afford        5     0.25
3 educ          3     0.15

Before imputation I am missing data on 3 variables: insur, afford, and educ.

Code 
brfss_log_imp <- 
  mice(brfss_2024, m = 1, seed = 432, print = FALSE) |>
  complete() |>
  tibble()
Warning: Number of logged events: 1
Code 
n_miss(brfss_log_imp)
[1] 0

After imputation we no longer have any missing values.

9.2 Model Y

We’ll predict Pr(flushot = 1), the probability of having a flu shot in the last 12 months, as a function of age, insur, educ, rural and afford.

9.2.1 Fitting Model Y

Code 
modelY_glm <- glm(flushot ~ age + insur + educ + rural + afford,
            data = brfss_log_imp, family = binomial())

ddd <- datadist(brfss_log_imp)
options(datadist = "ddd")

modelY_lrm <- lrm(flushot ~ age + insur + educ + rural + afford,
            data = brfss_log_imp, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

We fit with both lrm and glm so we can use them for different functions later on.

9.2.2 Tidied Odds Ratio Estimates (Model Y)

Code 
model_parameters(modelY_glm, ci = 0.90, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  print_md(digits = 3)
Parameter Odds Ratio SE 90% CI z p
(Intercept) 0.072 0.020 (0.045, 0.113) -9.415 < .001
age 1.033 0.004 (1.026, 1.039) 8.234 < .001
insur (Medicare) 1.076 0.141 (0.867, 1.335) 0.559 0.576
insur (Medicaid) 1.037 0.193 (0.761, 1.405) 0.193 0.847
insur (Other) 0.799 0.134 (0.606, 1.051) -1.337 0.181
educ (Some_College) 1.241 0.153 (1.013, 1.520) 1.754 0.079
educ (College_Grad) 2.526 0.294 (2.087, 3.062) 7.951 < .001
rural (Urban) 1.311 0.237 (0.976, 1.769) 1.497 0.134
afford (Yes) 0.674 0.130 (0.489, 0.921) -2.050 0.040

We exponentiate the coefficients so that we can look at the odds ratios.

9.2.3 Effects Plot (Model Y)

Code 
plot(summary(modelY_lrm, conf.int = 0.90))

From this, it looks like age and educ are having some of the strongest effects on our outcome flushot.

Code 
kable(summary(modelY_lrm, conf.int = 0.90), digits = 3)
Low High Diff. Effect S.E. Lower 0.9 Upper 0.9 Type
age 44 69 25 0.803 0.097 0.642 0.963 1
Odds Ratio 44 69 25 2.232 NA 1.901 2.620 2
insur - Medicare:Private 1 2 NA 0.073 0.131 -0.143 0.289 1
Odds Ratio 1 2 NA 1.076 NA 0.867 1.335 2
insur - Medicaid:Private 1 3 NA 0.036 0.186 -0.270 0.342 1
Odds Ratio 1 3 NA 1.037 NA 0.763 1.408 2
insur - Other:Private 1 4 NA -0.224 0.167 -0.499 0.051 1
Odds Ratio 1 4 NA 0.799 NA 0.607 1.053 2
educ - HS_or_less:College_Grad 3 1 NA -0.926 0.117 -1.118 -0.735 1
Odds Ratio 3 1 NA 0.396 NA 0.327 0.480 2
educ - Some_College:College_Grad 3 2 NA -0.710 0.121 -0.909 -0.511 1
Odds Ratio 3 2 NA 0.491 NA 0.403 0.600 2
rural - Rural:Urban 2 1 NA -0.270 0.181 -0.568 0.027 1
Odds Ratio 2 1 NA 0.763 NA 0.567 1.027 2
afford - Yes:No 1 2 NA -0.394 0.192 -0.710 -0.078 1
Odds Ratio 1 2 NA 0.674 NA 0.492 0.925 2

Looking at the variable age, if we have two subjects with the same values of insur, educ, rural and afford, but subject A has an age of 44 and subject B has an age of 69, our model Y predicts that the odds of subject B having our outcome flushot will be 2.232 times the odds of subject A having the outcome flushot. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with that odds ratio comparing A to B falling between 1.901 and 2.620, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model Y is correct.

9.2.4 Summarizing Fit (Model Y)

Code 
modelY_lrm
Logistic Regression Model

lrm(formula = flushot ~ age + insur + educ + rural + afford, 
    data = brfss_log_imp, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

                       Model Likelihood       Discrimination    Rank Discrim.    
                             Ratio Test              Indexes          Indexes    
Obs          2000    LR chi2     215.96       R2       0.137    C       0.692    
 0           1098    d.f.             8      R2(8,2000)0.099    Dxy     0.384    
 1            902    Pr(> chi2) <0.0001    R2(8,1485.6)0.131    gamma   0.385    
max |deriv| 3e-08                             Brier    0.221    tau-a   0.190    

                  Coef    S.E.   Wald Z Pr(>|Z|)
Intercept         -2.6370 0.2801 -9.41  <0.0001 
age                0.0321 0.0039  8.23  <0.0001 
insur=Medicare     0.0734 0.1313  0.56  0.5762  
insur=Medicaid     0.0360 0.1860  0.19  0.8467  
insur=Other       -0.2238 0.1673 -1.34  0.1811  
educ=Some_College  0.2161 0.1232  1.75  0.0795  
educ=College_Grad  0.9265 0.1165  7.95  <0.0001 
rural=Urban        0.2705 0.1807  1.50  0.1344  
afford=Yes        -0.3939 0.1922 -2.05  0.0404

Our Nagelkerke R2 estimate for model Y is 0.137 and our C statistic estimate is 0.692. Neither of these are that exciting, but our model is guessing better than random chance as our C statistic is greater than 0.5.

Code 
glance(modelY_glm) |>
  mutate(df = nobs - df.residual - 1) |>
  select(AIC, BIC, df, df.residual, nobs) |>
  kable(digits = 1)
AIC BIC df df.residual nobs
2555.4 2605.8 8 1991 2000

The AIC and BIC are not helpful right now, but they will allow us to compare models later. This also allows us to easily see that we have 8 degrees of freedom in model Y.

9.2.5 Confusion Matrix (Model Y)

Code 
modelY_aug <- augment(modelY_glm, type.predict = "response")

cp1 <- cutpointr(data = modelY_aug, .fitted, flushot, 
                 pos_class = 1, neg_class = 0,
                 method = maximize_metric, metric = sum_sens_spec)
Assuming the positive class has higher x values
Code 
cp1 |> select(direction, optimal_cutpoint, method, sum_sens_spec) |> kable(digits = 3)
direction optimal_cutpoint method sum_sens_spec
>= 0.406 maximize_metric 1.299

Using cutpointr, the decision rule that maximizes sensitivity and specificity is approximately 0.406. We will be using this cut point for both model Y and model Z, so we will round this down to 0.4. This means my prediction rule for this confusion matrix is that the fitted value of Pr(flushot = 1) needs to be greater than or equal to 0.4 for me to predict flushot is 1, and otherwise I predict 0.

Code 
cm1 <- confusionMatrix(data = factor(modelY_aug$.fitted >= 0.4),
                       reference = factor(modelY_aug$flushot == 1),
                       positive = "TRUE")
cm1
Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction FALSE TRUE
     FALSE   557  196
     TRUE    541  706
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.6315          
                 95% CI : (0.6099, 0.6527)
    No Information Rate : 0.549           
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 4.687e-14       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.2804          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.7827          
            Specificity : 0.5073          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.5662          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.7397          
             Prevalence : 0.4510          
         Detection Rate : 0.3530          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.6235          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6450          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : TRUE
Model Classification Rule Sensitivity Specificity Pos. Pred. Value
Y Predicted Pr(flushot = 1) >= 0.4 0.7827 0.5073 0.5662

9.3 Non-Linearity

Code 
plot(spearman2(flushot ~ age + insur + educ + rural + afford,
            data = brfss_log_imp))

The Spearman \(\rho^2\) plot suggests the use of non-linear terms with age and educ. As such, I will add a 5-knot restricted cubic spline to age, which has 4 degrees of freedom, and an interaction between age and educ which will add 2 more degrees of freedom.

9.4 Model Z

Model Z will contain two non-linear terms, adding up to an additional 5 degrees of freedom, to model Y.

9.4.1 Fitting Model Z

Code 
modelZ_glm <- glm(flushot ~ rcs(age, 5) + insur + educ + rural + afford + age %ia% educ,
                  data = brfss_log_imp, family = binomial())

ddd <- datadist(brfss_log_imp)
options(datadist = "ddd")

modelZ_lrm <- lrm(flushot ~ rcs(age, 5) + insur + educ + rural + afford + age %ia% educ,
                 data = brfss_log_imp, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

9.4.2 Tidied Odds Ratio Estimates (Model Z)

Code 
tidy(modelZ_glm, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.90) |>
  select(term, estimate, se = std.error, 
         low90 = conf.low, high90 = conf.high, p = p.value) |>
  kable(digits = 3)
term estimate se low90 high90 p
(Intercept) 0.268 0.557 0.105 0.659 0.018
rcs(age, 5)age 0.984 0.016 0.959 1.010 0.297
rcs(age, 5)age’ 1.118 0.046 1.036 1.206 0.016
rcs(age, 5)age’’ 0.770 0.286 0.481 1.232 0.361
rcs(age, 5)age’’’ 0.858 0.779 0.238 3.089 0.844
insurMedicare 0.839 0.151 0.654 1.074 0.244
insurMedicaid 1.067 0.191 0.778 1.458 0.734
insurOther 0.728 0.173 0.546 0.965 0.066
educSome_College 2.263 0.494 1.002 5.105 0.098
educCollege_Grad 8.336 0.448 4.018 17.580 0.000
ruralUrban 1.289 0.184 0.955 1.748 0.167
affordYes 0.690 0.194 0.499 0.945 0.055
age %ia% educage * educ=Some_College 0.990 0.008 0.977 1.004 0.234
age %ia% educage * educ=College_Grad 0.980 0.007 0.968 0.992 0.007

Once again, we exponentiate the coefficients so that we can look at the odds ratios.

9.4.3 Effects Plot (Model Z)

Code 
plot(summary(modelZ_lrm, conf.int = 0.90))

It looks like age is having a much greater effect on flushot then the rest of the variables now.

Code 
summary(modelZ_lrm, conf.int = 0.90) |> kable(digits = 3)
Low High Diff. Effect S.E. Lower 0.9 Upper 0.9 Type
age 44 69 25 1.189 0.194 0.870 1.508 1
Odds Ratio 44 69 25 3.282 NA 2.386 4.516 2
insur - Medicare:Private 1 2 NA -0.176 0.151 -0.423 0.072 1
Odds Ratio 1 2 NA 0.839 NA 0.655 1.075 2
insur - Medicaid:Private 1 3 NA 0.065 0.191 -0.249 0.379 1
Odds Ratio 1 3 NA 1.067 NA 0.780 1.460 2
insur - Other:Private 1 4 NA -0.318 0.173 -0.602 -0.034 1
Odds Ratio 1 4 NA 0.728 NA 0.548 0.967 2
educ - HS_or_less:College_Grad 3 1 NA -0.926 0.120 -1.124 -0.728 1
Odds Ratio 3 1 NA 0.396 NA 0.325 0.483 2
educ - Some_College:College_Grad 3 2 NA -0.683 0.123 -0.886 -0.480 1
Odds Ratio 3 2 NA 0.505 NA 0.412 0.619 2
rural - Rural:Urban 2 1 NA -0.254 0.184 -0.556 0.048 1
Odds Ratio 2 1 NA 0.776 NA 0.574 1.049 2
afford - Yes:No 1 2 NA -0.372 0.194 -0.691 -0.052 1
Odds Ratio 1 2 NA 0.690 NA 0.501 0.949 2

Looking at the variable age, if we have two subjects with the same values of insur, educ, rural and afford, but subject A has an age of 44 and subject B has an age of 69, our model Z predicts that the odds of subject B having our outcome flushot will be 3.282 times the odds of subject A having the outcome flushot. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with that odds ratio comparing A to B falling between 2.386 and 4.516, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model Y is correct.

9.4.4 Summarizing Fit (Model Z)

Code 
modelZ_lrm
Logistic Regression Model

lrm(formula = flushot ~ rcs(age, 5) + insur + educ + rural + 
    afford + age %ia% educ, data = brfss_log_imp, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)

                       Model Likelihood        Discrimination    Rank Discrim.    
                             Ratio Test               Indexes          Indexes    
Obs          2000    LR chi2     247.96        R2       0.156    C       0.696    
 0           1098    d.f.            13      R2(13,2000)0.111    Dxy     0.393    
 1            902    Pr(> chi2) <0.0001    R2(13,1485.6)0.146    gamma   0.394    
max |deriv| 3e-06                              Brier    0.218    tau-a   0.195    

                        Coef    S.E.   Wald Z Pr(>|Z|)
Intercept               -1.3182 0.5572 -2.37  0.0180  
age                     -0.0163 0.0156 -1.04  0.2966  
age'                     0.1111 0.0461  2.41  0.0159  
age''                   -0.2614 0.2862 -0.91  0.3611  
age'''                  -0.1534 0.7791 -0.20  0.8439  
insur=Medicare          -0.1755 0.1506 -1.17  0.2437  
insur=Medicaid           0.0647 0.1908  0.34  0.7343  
insur=Other             -0.3179 0.1727 -1.84  0.0656  
educ=Some_College        0.8167 0.4943  1.65  0.0985  
educ=College_Grad        2.1206 0.4483  4.73  <0.0001 
rural=Urban              0.2539 0.1836  1.38  0.1667  
afford=Yes              -0.3717 0.1941 -1.92  0.0555  
age * educ=Some_College -0.0097 0.0082 -1.19  0.2342  
age * educ=College_Grad -0.0202 0.0075 -2.71  0.0068

It looks like our Nagelkerke R2 estimate for model Z is 0.156 and our C statistic is 0.696. Both of these are a little bit higher than model Z, but still not too exciting.

Code 
glance(modelZ_glm) |>
  mutate(df = nobs - df.residual - 1) |>
  select(AIC, BIC, df, df.residual, nobs) |>
  kable(digits = 1)
AIC BIC df df.residual nobs
2533.4 2611.8 13 1986 2000

We will use the AIC and BIC later for selecting our final model. We can also easily see that we have 13 degrees of freedom, 5 more than model Y.

9.4.5 Confusion Matrix (Model Z)

As in model Y, my prediction rule for this confusion matrix is that the fitted value of Pr(flushot = 1) needs to be greater than or equal to 0.4 for me to predict flushot is 1, and otherwise I predict 0.

Code 
modelZ_aug <- augment(modelZ_glm, type.predict = "response")

cm2 <- confusionMatrix(data = factor(modelZ_aug$.fitted >= 0.4),
                       reference = factor(modelZ_aug$flushot == 1),
                       positive = "TRUE")
cm2
Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction FALSE TRUE
     FALSE   527  178
     TRUE    571  724
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.6255          
                 95% CI : (0.6039, 0.6468)
    No Information Rate : 0.549           
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 2.585e-12       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.272           
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.8027          
            Specificity : 0.4800          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.5591          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.7475          
             Prevalence : 0.4510          
         Detection Rate : 0.3620          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.6475          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6413          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : TRUE
Model Classification Rule Sensitivity Specificity Pos. Pred. Value
Z Predicted Pr(flushot = 1) >= 0.4 0.8027 0.4800 0.5591

9.5 Validating Models Y and Z

Code 
set.seed(4323); (valY <- validate(modelY_lrm, method = "boot", B = 300))
          index.orig training    test optimism index.corrected   Lower  Upper
Dxy           0.3836   0.3878  0.3767   0.0110          0.3725  0.3227 0.4178
R2            0.1369   0.1416  0.1326   0.0089          0.1280  0.0915 0.1602
Intercept     0.0000   0.0000 -0.0044   0.0044         -0.0044 -0.1285 0.1112
Slope         1.0000   1.0000  0.9666   0.0334          0.9666  0.7892 1.1603
Emax          0.0000   0.0000  0.0227  -0.0227          0.0227 -0.0037 0.0556
D             0.1075   0.1115  0.1039   0.0075          0.0999  0.0691 0.1267
U            -0.0010  -0.0010  0.0003  -0.0013          0.0003 -0.0018 0.0046

g             0.8112   0.8280  0.7959   0.0322          0.7790  0.6459 0.8974

U         300

          index.orig training    test optimism index.corrected   Lower  Upper
Dxy           0.3930   0.4023  0.3864   0.0159          0.3771  0.3345 0.4229

Intercept     0.0000   0.0000 -0.0068   0.0068         -0.0068 -0.1260 0.1078
Slope         1.0000   1.0000  0.9461   0.0539          0.9461  0.8075 1.1210

D             0.1235   0.1299  0.1176   0.0123          0.1112  0.0832 0.1407

Q             0.1245   0.1309  0.1172   0.0137          0.1108  0.0792 0.1409

            n
Dxy       300
R2        300
Intercept 300
Slope     300
Emax      300
D         300
U         300
Q         300
B         300
g         300
gp        300

9.5.1 Validated \(R^2\) and \(C\) statistics for each model

Model Validated \(R^2\) Validated C AIC BIC df
Y 0.128 0.6863 2555.4 2605.8 8
Z 0.1415 0.6885 2533.4 2611.8 13

9.6 Final Logistic Regression Model

I prefer model Z because it has a higher validated R2 and validated C statistic, as well as a lower AIC and higher sensitivity. The models perform very similarly, with no model completely dominating the other in all our measures, but there is a bit more of a difference between models for R2 and sensitivity, that makes model Z a compelling choice. I would probably be happy to use either, as I am not sure that the small difference is worth using the more complicated model, but I will still go with model Z.

9.6.1 Winning Model’s Parameter Estimates

Code 
tidy(modelZ_glm, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE, conf.level = 0.90) |>
  select(term, estimate, se = std.error, 
         low90 = conf.low, high90 = conf.high, p = p.value) |>
  kable(digits = 3)
term estimate se low90 high90 p
(Intercept) 0.268 0.557 0.105 0.659 0.018
rcs(age, 5)age 0.984 0.016 0.959 1.010 0.297
rcs(age, 5)age’ 1.118 0.046 1.036 1.206 0.016
rcs(age, 5)age’’ 0.770 0.286 0.481 1.232 0.361
rcs(age, 5)age’’’ 0.858 0.779 0.238 3.089 0.844
insurMedicare 0.839 0.151 0.654 1.074 0.244
insurMedicaid 1.067 0.191 0.778 1.458 0.734
insurOther 0.728 0.173 0.546 0.965 0.066
educSome_College 2.263 0.494 1.002 5.105 0.098
educCollege_Grad 8.336 0.448 4.018 17.580 0.000
ruralUrban 1.289 0.184 0.955 1.748 0.167
affordYes 0.690 0.194 0.499 0.945 0.055
age %ia% educage * educ=Some_College 0.990 0.008 0.977 1.004 0.234
age %ia% educage * educ=College_Grad 0.980 0.007 0.968 0.992 0.007

It looks like the educ levels have some of the bigger odds ratios.

9.6.2 Plot of Effect Sizes for Winning Model

Code 
plot(summary(modelZ_lrm, conf.int = 0.90))

Here it is easier to see the effect of age since it isn’t divided into degrees like with our coefficients. age is definitely having the largest effect on our model.

9.6.3 Numerical Description of Effect Sizes

Code 
summary(modelZ_lrm, conf.int = 0.90) |> kable(digits = 3)
Low High Diff. Effect S.E. Lower 0.9 Upper 0.9 Type
age 44 69 25 1.189 0.194 0.870 1.508 1
Odds Ratio 44 69 25 3.282 NA 2.386 4.516 2
insur - Medicare:Private 1 2 NA -0.176 0.151 -0.423 0.072 1
Odds Ratio 1 2 NA 0.839 NA 0.655 1.075 2
insur - Medicaid:Private 1 3 NA 0.065 0.191 -0.249 0.379 1
Odds Ratio 1 3 NA 1.067 NA 0.780 1.460 2
insur - Other:Private 1 4 NA -0.318 0.173 -0.602 -0.034 1
Odds Ratio 1 4 NA 0.728 NA 0.548 0.967 2
educ - HS_or_less:College_Grad 3 1 NA -0.926 0.120 -1.124 -0.728 1
Odds Ratio 3 1 NA 0.396 NA 0.325 0.483 2
educ - Some_College:College_Grad 3 2 NA -0.683 0.123 -0.886 -0.480 1
Odds Ratio 3 2 NA 0.505 NA 0.412 0.619 2
rural - Rural:Urban 2 1 NA -0.254 0.184 -0.556 0.048 1
Odds Ratio 2 1 NA 0.776 NA 0.574 1.049 2
afford - Yes:No 1 2 NA -0.372 0.194 -0.691 -0.052 1
Odds Ratio 1 2 NA 0.690 NA 0.501 0.949 2

Looking at the variable educ, if we have two subjects with the same values of insur, age, rural and afford, but subject A has an educ of ‘Some College’ and subject B has an educ of ‘College Grad’, our model Z predicts that the odds of subject A having our outcome flushot will be 0.505 times the odds of subject B having the outcome flushot. In addition, our data are consistent at the 90% confidence level with that odds ratio comparing A to B falling between 0.412 and 0.619, assuming both that our data are a random sample of the population of interest, and that our model Z is correct.

9.6.4 Plot of ROC Curve for Winning Model

Code 
roc.modelZ <- 
    roc(brfss_log_imp$flushot ~ predict(modelZ_glm, type="response"),
        ci = TRUE)
Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1
Setting direction: controls < cases
Code 
plot(roc.modelZ)

Here we can see that our model performs better than random chance, however a C statistic of 0.696 is not very exciting. This also displays the C statistic in our training data, but the validated C statistic is 0.6885.

9.6.5 Validated \(R^2\) and \(C\) statistic for Winning Model

  • The validated R2 statistic for Model Z is 0.1415, and
  • The validated C statistic for Model Z is 0.6885.

9.6.6 Nomogram of Winning Model

Code 
plot(nomogram(modelZ_lrm, fun = plogis, funlabel = "Pr(flushot = 1)"))

The nomogram shows us just how much more of an impact the variables of age and educ have than insur, rural and afford. We also get to see what the interaction between age and educ looks like, as model Z will predict a higher Pr(flushot=1) for subjects with higher education levels, even if they’re younger, but lower education levels have a much wider range depending on age.

I was surprised that educ ended up having a larger effect size than most of the other variables, as I expected insur or rural to have more of an impact on our outcome. I also found it interesting seeing what the non-linearity in our age variable looks like, as it decreased on the graph until around 40 for all education levels, before it begins to increase again.

9.6.7 Predictions for Two New Subjects

Code 
ggplot(Predict(modelZ_lrm, fun = plogis))

In our prediction plot we can see that the age variable took advantage of the non-linearity we added with a large dip around 40 years old. We can also see that the different levels of educ seem to have greater differences between levels than our other categorical variables.

Code 
new_subjects <- 
  data.frame(age = c(25,60),
             insur = c('Private', 'Private'),
             rural = c('Urban', 'Urban'),
             afford = c('Yes', 'Yes'),
             educ = c('College_Grad', 'HS_or_less')) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))

preds2 <- predict(modelZ_lrm, newdata = new_subjects, 
                  type = "fitted")

preds2
        1         2 
0.4429990 0.3050645
Subject age insur rural afford educ Pr(flushot = 1)
1 25 Private Urban Yes College Grad 0.443
2 60 Private Urban Yes HS or less 0.305

According to our decided prediction rule of Pr(flushot = 1) >= 0.4 for me to predict flushot is 1, subject 1 is predicted to have a flu shot while subject 2 is predicted to not have a flu shot.

10 Discussion

10.1 Answering My Research Questions

My first research question for my linear regression was:

How well can you predict the number of poor mental health days someone has had using demographic factors such as sex and age, and health factors like self-reported overall health, BMI, and previous depression diagnosis, in a sample of 679 subjects from the BRFSS 2024?

I would say that we cannot predict the number of poor mental health days all that well. Our validated R2 value for our final model A, was only 0.1345 meaning the variables we picked did not account for much of the variation in our data. In addition, when we predicted the outcome for a new subject, the 90% prediction interval covered nearly the entire range of values of our outcome. Together, this tells me that the variables I picked were not as associated with my outcome as much as I thought they would be. For further exploration, perhaps a different data set with more variables about mental health could help me create a better model than the BRFSS data set, which is more general and has questions across a number of topics.

My second research question for my logistic regression was:

What variables have the largest effect size when predicting whether or not a person has had a flu shot, from the variables of age, education level, living in a rural or urban county, insurance type, and whether the person can afford to see a doctor, in a sample of 2000 subjects from the 2024 BRFSS?

The variables that ended up with the largest effect size in our final model Z was first age, then education level. Age did not surprise me, as older people are usually encouraged to get flu shots more than younger people due to risk of more serious complications. Education level did surprise me however, as I thought there would be a difference between education levels, but I thought variables like type of insurance or living in an urban county would have greater effect sizes than education level. I would be interested to explore the relationship between my outcome and education levels further, mainly because I had to collapse my levels more than I would have liked to meet the 30 observations in each category rule. With a larger data set I could have more observations in each category and could look more at the differences between people who graduated high school and those that only attended some high school, for example.

10.2 Thoughts on Project A

Project A was the difficulty I expected it to be from my experience with projects in 431, in that I felt like I had all the tools in my belt to create visuals and statistical summaries, but it still took decent thought to interpret them. I also had to be careful to pay attention to details and look over my work over and over again, which is not difficult, but is time consuming and can get tiring when you have been staring at code for hours. Along with that, I wish I had spaced out my time better at the start of the project, so I wasn’t looking at code for so long. I didn’t feel like I rushed things or didn’t give myself enough time, but I would have liked to space out my work more, so I didn’t get as tired during my work. I find staring at numbers for hours on end doesn’t help my interpretation as much as giving myself a day to think more about it, which usually leads to more confidence in my interpretations.

11 Affirmation

I am certain that it is completely appropriate for these data to be shared with anyone, without any conditions. There are no concerns about privacy or security.

12 References

  1. The BRFSS 2024 data can be found at https://www.cdc.gov/brfss/annual_data/annual_2024.html

13 Use of AI

AI supported the development of this project through R Studio’s spell-check and its help with filling in the details of R functions.
